comments:
This paper studies learning neural semantic parsers from indirect supervision and makes a clear connection between reinforcement learning (policy-gradient/REINFORCE) and maximum marginal likelihood (MML). Building on that analysis, the authors propose RANDOMER, which (1) uses a randomized (epsilon-greedy) beam search to improve exploration and (2) applies a β-meritocratic gradient reweighting that more uniformly upweights all reward-earning programs. They implement a stack-based neural parser and evaluate on the SCONE dataset (three domains), reporting substantial gains over REINFORCE, beam-search MML, and prior state-of-the-art (Long et al. 2016), especially on the challenging SCENE domain. The authors also release code and provide sensible experimental details.

Strengths:
- Clear and useful conceptual link between RL and MML objectives and their gradients, which motivates the proposed remedies.
- Two simple, well-motivated algorithmic contributions (randomized beam search and β-meritocratic updates) that are easy to implement and integrate with beam-based training.
- Solid empirical results showing substantial accuracy improvements on a standard context-dependent semantic parsing benchmark. Good ablation studies (beam randomness, β values, execution-history embeddings) and consideration of training stability.
- Code release improves reproducibility.

Weaknesses / points for improvement:
- Evaluation is limited to SCONE domains. It would strengthen the paper to show transfer to additional semantic parsing or program-synthesis tasks (even smaller scoped ones) to demonstrate broader applicability.
- The β hyperparameter can be important; more guidance on how to anneal or select β automatically would be useful. The paper notes annealing might help but does not explore it in depth.
- Theoretical properties are informal; no convergence/variance analysis is provided. While not required, a more thorough analysis of when and why β-meritocratic updates help (beyond empirical evidence) would be welcome.
- Comparison to other modern exploration techniques (e.g., actor-critic, entropy regularization, Monte-Carlo tree search) is discussed qualitatively but not empirically; including one such baseline could strengthen claims about randomized beam search being preferable in this setting.

Overall, this is a well-written, thoughtful paper with practical contributions and convincing empirical gains. I recommend acceptance / strong endorsement for presentation.
